was one of the earliest [bullpup](/wiki/Bullpup "Bullpup") [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle"), developed by an English gunsmith in 1901 as patent No. 14,622 of July 18, 1901. This [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt_action "Bolt action") rifle featured a bullpup action in which the retracted bolt slid back through the stock nearly to the shooter's shoulder, maximising the space available in the body of the firearm. The rifle was chambered in the contemporary [.303 British](/wiki/.303_British ".303 British") service cartridge, and held five rounds in an internal magazine.

Please help [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Fact_and_Reference_Check "Wikipedia:WikiProject Fact and Reference Check") this article by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(November 2014)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Bullpup bolt-action rifle

| Thorneycroft carbine | |
| --- | --- |
| Drawings from original patent | |
| Type | [Bullpup](/wiki/Bullpup "Bullpup") [bolt-action rifle](/wiki/Bolt-action_rifle "Bolt-action rifle") |
| Place of origin | United Kingdom |
| Production history | |
| Designed | 1901 |
| Specifications | |
| [Cartridge](/wiki/Cartridge_(firearms) "Cartridge (firearms)") | [.303 British](/wiki/.303_British ".303 British") |
| [Action](/wiki/Action_(firearms) "Action (firearms)") | [Bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt-action "Bolt-action") |
| Feed system | 5-round internal magazine |
| Sights | [Iron sights](/wiki/Iron_sight "Iron sight") |

The **Thorneycroft carbine** was one of the earliest [bullpup](/wiki/Bullpup "Bullpup") [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle"), developed by an English gunsmith in 1901 as patent No. 14,622 of July 18, 1901. This [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt_action "Bolt action") rifle featured a bullpup action in which the retracted bolt slid back through the stock nearly to the shooter's shoulder, maximising the space available in the body of the firearm. The rifle was chambered in the contemporary [.303 British](/wiki/.303_British ".303 British") service cartridge, and held five rounds in an internal magazine.

The Thorneycroft was 7.5 in (190 mm) shorter and 10% lighter than the standard [Lee–Enfield rifle](/wiki/Lee%E2%80%93Enfield_rifle "Lee–Enfield rifle") used by the British military at the time. However, when tested at [Hythe](/wiki/Hythe,_Kent "Hythe, Kent") the firearm exhibited excessive recoil and poor ergonomics, and was not adopted for military service.
